summary: Feature request to expose Skia's SkPath operator== as SKPath.Equals(SKPath) in the C# API, enabling efficient native path comparison instead of iterating verbs in managed code.

repoLinks.description: Skia PathKit docs showing equals(otherPath) method

analysis.summary: Well-scoped feature request. Skia C++ has SkPath::operator== which compares FillType, verb array, point array, and weights. The C API (sk_path.h) does not expose an equality function, and SKPath in C# has no Equals override. Implementation would require adding sk_path_equals to the C API, regenerating bindings, and adding Equals/IEquatable<SKPath> to the C# wrapper.
analysis.rationale: This is a feature request (not enhancement) because no path comparison API exists at any layer of the SkiaSharp stack. The upstream C++ supports it natively via operator==, so implementation is straightforward plumbing through C API and C# layers. The reporter's alternative of iterating verbs in C# is valid but significantly slower due to P/Invoke overhead per verb.

keySignals.text: Skia has an equals(otherPath) method that would be good to expose
keySignals.interpretation: Reporter correctly identifies the upstream capability and requests it be exposed.

keySignals.text: Iterating over the paths in C# and looking for differences, but that would be considerably slower
keySignals.interpretation: Valid concern — verb-by-verb iteration via P/Invoke is O(n) with per-call overhead vs single native comparison.

codeInvestigation.finding: SkPath has friend operator== that compares FillType, verb array, SkPoint array, and weights. Also has operator!=. This is the upstream API the reporter wants exposed.
No sk_path_equals or similar function exists in the C API header. The equality comparison is not plumbed through the C shim layer.
SKPath class does not implement IEquatable<SKPath> and has no Equals override. No equality comparison is available in managed code.
SKPath exposes CreateIterator and CreateRawIterator which could be used for manual verb-by-verb comparison as the reporter's workaround, though with significant P/Invoke overhead.
SKPath.ToSvgPathData() converts path to SVG string — could serve as an approximate equality check via string comparison, but loses fill type, conic weights, and floating-point precision.

workarounds: Compare paths approximately via SVG strings: path1.ToSvgPathData() == path2.ToSvgPathData() — note this loses fill type, conic weights, and floating-point precision beyond 6 significant digits
Iterate both paths with CreateRawIterator() and compare verbs, points, and conic weights manually (correct but slow due to P/Invoke per verb)
nextQuestions: Should SKPath implement IEquatable<SKPath> with operator== and operator!= in addition to Equals?
Should GetHashCode also be overridden for consistency (SkPath doesn't expose a hash function)?

resolution.hypothesis: The C++ SkPath::operator== exists and works correctly. Adding the C API function and C# wrapper is a straightforward 3-layer plumbing task.

proposals.title: SVG string comparison workaround
proposals.description: Compare paths via their SVG string representation. Quick to implement now but lossy: SVG does not encode fill type, converts conics to quadratics (losing weights), and uses limited floating-point precision (~6 significant digits with %g format). Only suitable for approximate visual comparison of simple paths.
proposals.codeSnippet: bool AreApproximatelyEqual(SKPath a, SKPath b) => a.ToSvgPathData() == b.ToSvgPathData();

proposals.title: Add sk_path_equals to C API and SKPath.Equals to C#
proposals.description: Add sk_path_equals(const sk_path_t*, const sk_path_t*) to C API wrapping SkPath::operator==, regenerate bindings, then add Equals(SKPath) and IEquatable<SKPath> to the C# wrapper with operator== and operator!= overloads.

resolution.recommendedProposal: Add sk_path_equals to C API and SKPath.Equals to C#
resolution.recommendedReason: Straightforward implementation since the upstream C++ API already exists. Provides the most efficient and correct comparison, preserving fill type, conic weights, and full floating-point precision.

actionability.reason: Well-scoped feature request with clear implementation path through all three layers (C API, bindings, C# wrapper). Upstream Skia supports it natively.

actions.description: Acknowledge feature request and explain implementation path
actions.comment:
Thanks for the request — this is a reasonable API to expose since Skia's C++ `SkPath::operator==` already supports it, comparing fill type, verb array, point coordinates, and conic weights.

The iterator-based workaround you mentioned is the most correct managed-code approach. An SVG-based comparison (`ToSvgPathData()`) is simpler but lossy — it doesn't preserve fill type, converts conics to quadratics, and truncates coordinate precision.

The proper fix would be to plumb `SkPath::operator==` through the C API as `sk_path_equals` and expose it as `SKPath.Equals(SKPath)` with `IEquatable<SKPath>` support. This is a well-scoped addition.
